title: Pilosella floribunda
intro: Pilosella floribunda, also known as the clustered hawk's beard or woolly hawk's beard, is a flowering plant in the dandelion family (Asteraceae) characterized by its rosettes of woolly leaves and dense clusters of yellow flowers. The plant is valued for its potential health benefits, including anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which may help to soothe digestive issues and reduce the severity of colds and flu. The bioactive constituents of Pilosella floribunda include sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which are thought to contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional herbalism, Pilosella floribunda is often used to make teas, tinctures, and infusions, which are used to support digestive health and boost the immune system.
taxonomy: Pilosella floribunda belongs to the family Asteraceae, commonly known as the daisy or sunflower family. It is a member of the subfamily Cichorioideae, which includes plants with composite flowers. Further classification places it in the tribe Cichorieae, a group of plants characterized by their leafy, often thistle-like stems. Within this tribe, Pilosella floribunda can be placed in the subtribe Pilosellinae, a group of plants closely related to the genus Cichorium. Its species classification places it in the genus Pilosella, which is primarily composed of plants commonly known as fleabanes.
uses: Pilosella floribunda has been traditionally used in folk medicine to treat various ailments, including digestive issues and skin problems. Its leaves and stems contain compounds with potential anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Traditionally, the plant has been used to prepare infusions, decoctions, and even salves to alleviate symptoms. However, more research is needed to confirm the effectiveness and safety of these uses. It is essential to consult with a healthcare professional before using any plant-based remedies.

preparations: Pilosella floribunda is not commonly used in herbal preparations, and there is limited information available on its traditional or modern uses. However, its close relative Pilosella officinarum, also known as meadow-sorrel or oxalis, has been used in herbal medicine for various purposes. The plant's leaves and stems are said to have a sour taste and contain oxalic acid, which has been used as a diuretic and to treat skin conditions.
